## Space and Momentum: Stage and Actor

Before turning to clocks and dilation, we need one further stance on space and momentum.

### Space as the stage

In M1, space is the stage on which physical systems exist, persist, and change. This does not make it empty scenery. A stage has structure: it determines which relations are available, which configurations can be stable, and what counts as possible motion for a physical system.

If the stage is different, the same momentum content need not support the same trajectories, equilibria, or local kinematic behavior. In the inertial baseline, this stage is treated as given. Later, gravity will ask how organized momentum modifies the local stage. Here the needed point is simpler: space conditions the available kinematic relations.

### Momentum as the actor

Momentum is the actor of change. Translational motion is momentum expression. Mass is read through locked fermic momentum. Interaction is described through transfer, redistribution, or reorganization of momentum content.

This is the force of the momentum-first claim. What changes in a physical system changes through momentum expressed within spatial relations, not because time acts as an independent driver.

### Why the distinction matters

The stage and the actor are not separate worlds. Momentum is always expressed in space, and space matters because it conditions expression. But the role difference keeps the explanatory order clear: space supplies the relational setting; momentum carries the changing physical content.

A clock is a physical system undergoing regular change. If clock rates differ, the first place to look is not a changing time medium, but a difference in how the clock's momentum content is organized and expressed. The next section turns to time and dilation from that standpoint.
